# **Architectural Analysis of Terrain Fragment Shader Compositing for Beyond All Reason**

## **Engine Rendering Architecture and Shader Pathways**

The rendering architecture of the Recoil Engine, derived from the historical Spring 105 codebase, utilizes a highly dynamic, macro-driven shader compilation pipeline to manage terrain compositing. The terrain rendering subsystem is fundamentally anchored in the rts/Map/SMF/ directory, where the core logic coordinates the intersection of heightmap geometry, texture splatting, and environmental lighting.1 The primary control structure for rendering the ground plane resides within SMFGroundDrawer.cpp, a substantial module responsible for evaluating visibility, managing level-of-detail transitions, and binding the appropriate OpenGL render states prior to dispatching draw calls.1 This module works in tandem with the Real-time Optimally Adapting Meshes (ROAM) implementation, specifically Patch.cpp and RoamMeshDrawer.cpp, which dynamically tessellate the heightmap into optimized triangles based on camera distance and frustum culling.2  
The exact GLSL fragment shaders responsible for compositing the splat distribution and Detail Normal Texture Splatting (DNTS) are not stored as monolithic files. Instead, they are dynamically constructed strings that incorporate common base files and utility functions located in the cont/base/springcontent/shaders/GLSL/ directory.3 The engine uses a C++ component, SMFRenderState.cpp, to dictate the injection of preprocessor directives such as \#define USE\_NORMALMAP and \#define USE\_DIFFUSE\_ALPHA into the shader source before compilation. This macro injection effectively branches the shader pipeline based directly on the configuration found in the map's mapinfo.lua resource blocks.6  
Because of this architectural design, there is not a singular, uniform shader path that executes for all maps. The engine dynamically selects from three predominant operational branches based on the texture assets provided by the map author. The first branch is the legacy rendering pathway, which is triggered when the splatDetailNormalTex\[1-4\] array is entirely absent from the map configuration. This older path relies on a single, global splatDetailTex or a lower-frequency detailTex to provide noise and texture variation over the base terrain, bypassing complex normal calculations entirely.6  
The second branch is the standard DNTS compositing pathway. This is activated when the splatDetailNormalTex array is populated with up to four texture paths, and the splatDetailNormalDiffuseAlpha field is explicitly set to 0 or omitted entirely.6 In this configuration, the engine extracts tangent-space normal vectors from the RGB channels of the four DNTS textures. It treats the base diffuse map (derived from the SMT format) as the primary source of color, using the DNTS textures purely for high-frequency geometric detail and lighting perturbation.7  
The third branch, representing the most visually complex configuration, is the DNTS with alpha-modulated diffuse pathway. This branch executes when splatDetailNormalDiffuseAlpha \= 1 is declared in the configuration.7 This configuration fundamentally alters the shader's behavior by overriding the standard diffuse blending operation. The shader extracts high-pass filtered diffuse color data from the alpha channel of each of the four DNTS textures. This extracted color data is subsequently modulated and additively blended over the underlying base diffuse layer, allowing the splat maps to control both geometric normals and high-resolution color simultaneously.7  
A critical and historically documented architectural anomaly of the Recoil renderer involves a strict dependency on the specular texture configuration. Documentation and engine behavior dictate that the activation of the tangent-space normal mapping pathways silently fails or disables if a global specularTex is missing from the mapinfo.lua resources block.6 This behavior originates from the legacy fixed-function origins of the Spring engine, where the tangent binormal (TBN) matrix generation within the fragment shader required the specular lighting pass to be active to correctly calculate light reflection vectors.5 If specularTex is omitted, the engine reverts to the legacy non-DNTS render path, entirely ignoring the provided normal textures despite their presence in the configuration file.7 This hard dependency dictates that map authors must provide a valid specular map to utilize advanced normal splatting.

## **Mathematics of Diffuse Compositing**

The construction of the final ground diffuse color within the fragment shader is an additive modulation process governed primarily by the splat distribution map. The base terrain color is derived from a massive texture pool constructed by the SMF format, utilizing SMT files.6 These SMT files provide a global, low-frequency base\_diffuse map constructed from deduplicated 32x32 pixel DXT1 compressed tiles.6 The shader samples this base texture first to establish the foundational color of the fragment.  
The distribution of high-frequency detail across the map is subsequently controlled by the splatDistrTex. This is typically an 8-bit per channel RGBA texture, provided in PNG, TGA, or DDS format, that maps identically to the terrain bounds.6 The four channels (Red, Green, Blue, Alpha) act as independent, grayscale distribution masks defining the spatial intensity of the four corresponding detail textures.7  
When evaluating a fragment, the shader reads the RGBA weights from the distribution texture. For a given pixel, the diffuse composite evaluates based on the splatDetailNormalDiffuseAlpha state. If this state is active, the alpha channel of each DNTS texture contains a diffuse overlay.6 The final diffuse color is calculated as an additive blend over the base texture, modulated by the channel intensities defined in the splats.texMults array 6:  
![][image1]  
In this mathematical formulation, ![][image2] represents the SMT-derived base texture sampled at the fragment's world coordinate. ![][image3] represents the continuous float values \[0.0, 1.0\] extracted from the splat distribution channels (R, G, B, A). ![][image4] is the sampled alpha value from the ![][image5]\-th DNTS texture, which carries the high-frequency color data. ![][image6] corresponds to the scalar array splats.texMults provided in the map configuration.6  
The explicit purpose of texMults\[i\] is purely scalar intensity amplification for the DNTS sample.6 It acts as a direct brightness and intensity multiplier, allowing map authors to artificially boost or suppress the visual impact of a specific terrain layer without requiring destructive edits to the underlying texture assets.7 For example, in the Orbital Station map configuration, these multipliers are set to variable values such as {0.5, 0.9, 0.01, 0.01}, radically altering the presence of the floor tiles versus the outside edge plates.6  
Crucially, the Recoil engine enforces no strict channel sum invariant on the distribution texture. If a pixel in the splatDistrTex contains a value of (255, 255, 255, 255), indicating maximum intensity across all four detail layers, the shader performs a raw mathematical sum of all layers.7 In the native engine, this lack of normalization leads to an additive saturation, effectively a white-out of the diffuse and normal structures.7 While map authors are expected to manually normalize their distribution maps during the authoring phase to prevent this blowout, the shader implementation itself performs no mathematical normalization (e.g., it does not divide the sampled weights by the total sum of the weights).  
In configurations where DNTS is disabled, or as a global macro-overlay, the detailTex plays a role.6 When splatDetailNormalTex is provided, the legacy splatDetailTex is entirely ignored by the engine.7 However, the global detailTex can remain active as a universal multiply-blend over the base color, providing high-frequency noise and visual variation even in regions where the distribution texture evaluates to zero, preventing the base SMT texture from appearing overly blurred upon close camera inspection.6

## **Tangent-Space Normal Synthesis and Blending**

The engine handles terrain normal mapping through a localized TBN (Tangent, Binormal, Normal) matrix constructed per-vertex within the ROAM mesh generation algorithm.2 The base terrain mesh inherently provides a ground\_base\_normal resulting from the heightmap's geometric gradient. The DNTS pipeline does not overwrite or replace this base normal; rather, it applies high-frequency detail deltas on top of it, creating the illusion of complex surface geometry.8  
The four DNTS textures are conventionally compressed using BC3 (DXT5) formats. To optimize memory bandwidth and reduce storage footprints, the engine extracts the X and Y tangent-space normal vectors from the Red and Green channels of these textures, respectively.7 The Z component is typically reconstructed in the shader using the standard formula ![][image7], assuming the normal vector has a length of one.  
Due to the OpenGL origins of the Recoil engine, the tangent space expects a Y-up coordinate system.7 Consequently, assets authored in DirectX conventions (such as standard AmbientCG material libraries or modern Substance Painter outputs) require an explicit inversion of the green channel to render correctly.8 This requirement is evidenced by the \_flipped nomenclature commonly found on shipping assets within the engine, such as Metal\_BrushedMetalTilesDirty\_2k\_dnts\_flipped.dds.6 Failure to invert this channel results in lighting artifacts where concavities appear as convexities and vice versa.  
The fragment shader composites the four individual DNTS normal samples utilizing the identical splatDistrTex RGBA weights that were used during the diffuse pass.7 The compositing mathematics rely on a linear weighted sum of the tangent-space vectors, followed by a final mathematical re-normalization to ensure the resulting vector maintains unit length:  
![][image8]  
In this equation, ![][image3] represents the distribution weight, and ![][image9] represents the normal data extracted from the texture channel. The multiplication by 2.0 and subtraction of 1.0 transforms the texture data from the \`\` color space into the \[-1, 1\] vector space required for directional math.  
This linear blending of normals in tangent space is mathematically imperfect compared to advanced techniques like Reoriented Normal Mapping (RNM) or spherical linear interpolation (slerp). Linear blending of non-collinear vectors inherently shortens the resulting vector length, which is why the final normalization step is strictly required. Despite its mathematical simplicity, this linear sum approach is highly performant and universally utilized in the Recoil terrain pipeline to composite up to four normal maps simultaneously in a single fragment pass.7 Once the blended tangent-space normal is derived, it is multiplied by the fragment's interpolated TBN matrix to yield the final world-space normal used for subsequent illumination equations.

## **Specular Illumination and Environmental Reflection Dynamics**

Specular highlights and environmental reflections are essential components of the rendering pipeline, defining the material characteristics of the terrain and preventing the map from appearing perfectly matte under direct sunlight.8 The engine relies heavily on the specularTex asset, which provides both the specular intensity and the specular exponent per-fragment.6  
The RGB channels of the specularTex define the color and magnitude of the reflection.8 This allows map authors to specify that certain materials, such as metallic ore deposits or wet sand, reflect light intensely, while surrounding dirt or grass remains diffuse. The Alpha channel of the specularTex is uniquely utilized to scale the base Phong exponent, effectively controlling the glossiness or "shininess" of the surface.8 A higher value in the alpha channel results in a tighter, sharper specular highlight, indicative of a smooth surface, while a lower value spreads the highlight out, representing rougher materials.6  
The exact illumination model utilized by the fragment shader follows a modified Blinn-Phong formulation.6 The specular term for any given fragment is calculated as:  
![][image10]  
Where ![][image11] corresponds to the global lighting.groundSpecularColor parameter defined in the map configuration.6 ![][image12] represents the sampled data from the specularTex.8 ![][image13] is the composited world-space normal vector calculated in the previous step, and ![][image14] is the half-vector calculated between the viewer's eye vector and the directional light source. ![][image15] is the global lighting.specularExponent.6  
When the skyReflectModTex is defined in the resources block, the engine leverages a pre-rendered cubemap to simulate environmental skybox reflections on the terrain surface.6 This technique is particularly effective for simulating standing water or highly polished metallic surfaces across the landscape. The engine computes a standard reflection vector ![][image16] utilizing the view vector ![][image17] and the composited normal ![][image13]. It then samples the cubemap using this reflection vector.6  
This cubemap sample is heavily modulated by the baseline specular intensity defined by the specularTex. The implementation in the Recoil engine acts as an additive environmental lobe rather than a sophisticated Physically Based Rendering (PBR) clearcoat layer or energy-conserving microfacet model.6 The shader merely sums the reflection output directly into the final fragment color based on the viewing angle and local specular mask.6 This approach is computationally inexpensive but can lead to energy violation if the map author provides excessively bright specular textures alongside high-intensity diffuse colors.8  
As previously established, the splatDetailNormalTex pipeline features a rigid dependency on the specularTex.7 If the specularTex file path is omitted or fails to load, the C++ rendering state manager completely bypasses the injection of the tangent-space normal blending code into the fragment shader.7 This architectural constraint forces authors to supply specular data if they intend to utilize the normal mapping features of the engine.

## **Coordinate Spaces, Scales, and UV Mapping**

The correct conversion and application of coordinate spaces across the various texture layers is paramount to replicating the visual output of the engine. The Recoil engine operates on fundamental units of distance termed "elmos," where one elmo equates to one baseline engine unit.8 A standard map dimension is traditionally defined in kilometers, where ![][image18] is strictly equivalent to exactly ![][image19].8 This scale dictates how UV coordinates are generated and mapped across the heightmap geometry.  
The coordinate spaces for the respective textures function in distinctly different manners based on their intended role:

1. **Base Diffuse (SMT)**: The primary UV space for the global terrain is strictly world-aligned. The base terrain tile is defined with a fixed resolution per Square Map Unit (SMU). The vertex shader computes the base UVs directly from the world coordinates, ensuring that the texture remains locked to the terrain geometry regardless of camera position or chunk rendering. The calculation is fundamentally ![][image20].  
2. **Splat Distribution (splatDistrTex)**: The distribution texture shares the exact same macroscopic UV coordinate space as the base diffuse texture.7 It stretches across the entire playable bounds of the map, resulting in a highly precise 1:1 mapping with the macroscopic heightmap geometry.7 Because of this global mapping, a single pixel in the distribution texture can cover a substantial physical area on large maps, making the blending transition relatively soft.  
3. **DNTS Layering (texScales)**: The four detailed normal textures utilize a completely independent, high-frequency UV scaling system derived from the splats.texScales array.6 This scale array dictates a multiplicative transformation applied to the base world coordinates, not a divisional one. The fragment UV for the ![][image5]\-th DNTS sample is mathematically derived as: ![][image21]. Because this is a multiplier, a parameter such as 0.00260416 (utilized in the Orbital Station map) evaluates to a fraction of approximately ![][image22].6 This translates to the texture tiling exactly once every ![][image23] elmos, generating a massive physical footprint for a single 2K detail texture. Conversely, a higher float value in the texScales array results in tighter, more repetitive tiling across the terrain.6 Map authors must carefully balance these scales to avoid obvious repeating patterns while maintaining sufficient detail.  
4. **Specular and Detail Textures**: Both the specularTex and the global detailTex typically utilize a scaled subset of the base UV coordinates, often repeating multiple times across a single SMU to provide high-frequency noise that breaks up the low-resolution nature of the SMT base textures.6

## **Global Illumination Integration and Attenuation**

The final integration of the fragment color occurs via a standard forward-rendering illumination model, combining ambient, directional, and specular light sources into a cohesive visual output:  
![][image24]  
In this equation, ![][image25] represents the environmental baseline illumination. It is purely a product of the global lighting.groundambientcolor vector defined in the map configuration 6, multiplied directly against the fragment's composited diffuse albedo. This ensures that even areas in complete shadow maintain some level of visibility and color accuracy.  
The primary directional light vector is provided by the lighting.sunDir parameter, which is structured as a four-component vector.6 The initial three components (x, y, z) dictate the directional vector of the celestial light source, pointing from the light source towards the origin.6 The Lambertian diffuse term (![][image26]) is calculated using the dot product of this directional vector and the composited world-space normal, scaled by the lighting.grounddiffusecolor.6  
The fourth component of the sunDir vector, w (often referred to in configuration as sunStart or sunStartAngle), is typically evaluated at extremely high values such as 1e9.6 In the context of the Spring 105 shader matrices and shadow mapping implementations, this component operates as a shadow projection boundary or an infinitely distant termination threshold for ray-marched sun calculations, rather than participating directly in the color vector mathematics for the ground plane.6  
Shadow mapping dictates the visibility term ![][image27]. The directional light terms—both the Lambertian diffuse and the Specular reflection—are linearly scaled by the absence of occlusion. The engine provides a groundShadowDensity uniform to control the opacity of the shadows cast across the terrain. The visibility is interpolated as ![][image28]. A shadow density of zero implies no shadows, while a value of 1.0 results in mathematically pure black shadows blocking all directional light.  
Interactions with engine voids, specifically voidWater or voidGround boundaries where the terrain drops below the defined minimum playable height, do not alter the fundamental illumination mathematics. Instead, they trigger early termination pathways within the fragment shader.5 This is typically achieved via an alpha test or a strict GLSL discard directive, completely culling the fragment from the depth and color framebuffers before the expensive illumination models are fully evaluated.5 This optimization prevents the engine from calculating advanced DNTS lighting on terrain that is hidden beneath opaque water layers or outside the map boundaries.

## **Strategic Render Simplifications for External Tooling**

When developing an external, standalone Rust and wgpu-based terrain editor designed to interface with these specific map archives, attempting to recreate the exhaustive Recoil C++ rendering pipeline pixel-for-pixel is entirely unnecessary for the user authoring phase. The required benchmark is "visually believable" splat compositing, enabling the user to evaluate the interaction of base textures, normals, and intensity scalars correctly while painting with splat brushes.  
To achieve an efficient, robust 3D preview within the strict architectural constraints of wgpu, specific mathematical and architectural simplifications should be enforced. These reductions maximize rendering performance while maintaining the core visual identity of the map.

1. **Exclusion of Environmental Cubemaps**: Handling the asynchronous loading, mip-generation, and memory management of the skyReflectModTex provides negligible benefit to the terrain texturing workflow. The cubemap sampling phase should be entirely excised from the editor shader. The editor should rely solely on the mathematically deterministic Blinn-Phong specular lobe derived from the specularTex.6  
2. **Linear Normal Summation without TBN Re-Orthogonalization**: The preview shader should linearly blend the four tangent space normals based on the distribution weights and normalize the final result. However, the accurate per-fragment TBN matrix generation based on precise ROAM mesh derivatives can be substituted with a simple geometric grid normal.2 Calculating complex terrain curvature derivatives in the vertex shader adds unnecessary overhead when flat-grid normals suffice for painting previews.  
3. **Static Ambient/Directional Scaffolding**: The editor should hardcode an ambient base and a single Lambertian directional light matching the mapinfo.lua sunDir vector 6, aggressively omitting complex shadow cascaded mapping and density modulation. Real-time shadow rendering distracts from analyzing the actual color and normal blends of the textures being painted.

Implementing these reductions will induce documented "preview drift," meaning the editor output will diverge in specific, predictable ways from the final in-engine frames rendered by Recoil. These deviations are acceptable but must be explicitly documented to manage user expectations. Documented drift items will include:

* The total absence of environmental skybox reflections on heavily specular terrain.6  
* A lack of dynamic self-shadowing from high-frequency terrain changes or placed units.  
* The omission of global atmospheric scattering, exponential distance fog, and cloud shadows.6  
* Minor discrepancies in normal vector interpolation due to differences between wgpu flat-grid rendering and the engine's dynamic LOD ROAM mesh patching.2  
* The absence of water plane intersection darkening and sub-surface terrain tinting.5

When sequencing the implementation of the editor shader, the foremost priority (the 80% perceptual match) consists of mapping the diffuse SMT base, modulating the alpha-diffuse layers via the splat distribution, and correctly scaling UVs via the texScales multipliers.6 Specular intensity overlays and advanced Phong exponent mapping should be deferred to secondary polish phases once the base diffuse pipeline is stable.

## **ADR-035 — Terrain fragment shader composite**

**Status:** Proposed (research) — 2026-05-18 **Context:** During the development of the standalone map editor (Sprint 9 F4-stage), the Splat Distribution painting toolset requires an accurate, real-time 3D preview of the composited terrain. In-game Recoil engine maps, such as Orbital Station and Enceladus, utilize a complex Detail Normal Texture Splatting (DNTS) pipeline driven by numerous mapinfo.lua parameters to composite up to four normal and diffuse layers over an SMT-backed base texture.6 Without a computationally faithful representation of this shader composite within our wgpu environment, users will inadvertently author flawed distribution maps that result in severe visual desynchronization when subsequently compiled and loaded into the Recoil engine.  
**Decision:** Adopt the composite mathematical formula derived directly from Recoil's dynamic SMFGroundFrag.glsl pipeline, heavily prioritizing the USE\_NORMALMAP and USE\_DIFFUSE\_ALPHA logic branches.4 The editor preview will implement exact splats.texScales multiplicative coordinate mapping 6, alpha-channel diffuse modulation (when flagged by the configuration) 7, and linear tangent-space normal blending.8 This will be combined with strategic editor-preview simplifications to guarantee highly performant wgpu execution while maintaining visual believability.  
**Alternatives considered:**

* Naïve "average DNTS by weight": rejected — this approach produces a mathematically muddy blend that severely misrepresents the additive saturation characteristics of the native engine, hiding potential authoring blowouts.7  
* PBR-like metallic/roughness rendering: rejected — this paradigm is thoroughly outside BAR's engine schema; mapping Recoil's legacy specular exponent data to a modern PBR roughness pipeline introduces visual hallucinations and material responses completely absent in the actual game.6  
* Sample only base\_diffuse, skip DNTS entirely in preview: rejected — entirely obscures user authoring intent regarding splat brush application and fine detail placement.6

**Consequences:**

* The wgpu fragment shader will necessitate a 6-texture bind group structure: the base SMT texture, the splat distribution texture, the four DNTS BC3 textures, and an optional specular mask. This comfortably resides within the max\_sampled\_textures\_per\_shader\_stage architectural limit of 16 enforced by wgpu.  
* The editor preview will experience intentional and documented visual drift from the in-engine final render. The primary drift vectors include the absence of skyReflectModTex environment mapping, the lack of real-time directional shadow mapping, the omission of atmospheric volume scattering, and minor normal-interpolation differences.6 These drifts will be explicitly communicated in the editor's About panel.  
* The splatDetailNormalTex dependency upon the existence of specularTex is an architectural limitation of the Spring engine and will be strictly validated by the internal linter (C8 / sprint 13\) prior to .sd7 archive exportation to prevent silent render failures.7

### **Composite math (the formula we'll write into terrain.wgsl)**

Code snippet

struct FragmentInput {  
    @location(0) world\_pos: vec3\<f32\>,  
    @location(1) uv\_base: vec2\<f32\>, // Calculated as world.xz / (map\_dims \* 512\)  
    @location(2) normal: vec3\<f32\>,  
    @location(3) tangent: vec3\<f32\>,  
    @location(4) bitangent: vec3\<f32\>,  
};

// Texture bindings accommodating the 6 required maps  
@group(0) @binding(0) var samp: sampler;  
@group(1) @binding(0) var t\_base: texture\_2d\<f32\>;  
@group(1) @binding(1) var t\_dist: texture\_2d\<f32\>;  
@group(1) @binding(2) var t\_dnts0: texture\_2d\<f32\>;  
@group(1) @binding(3) var t\_dnts1: texture\_2d\<f32\>;  
@group(1) @binding(4) var t\_dnts2: texture\_2d\<f32\>;  
@group(1) @binding(5) var t\_dnts3: texture\_2d\<f32\>;  
@group(1) @binding(6) var t\_spec: texture\_2d\<f32\>;

// Uniforms injected directly from parsed mapinfo.lua parameters  
struct MapInfoUniforms {  
    tex\_scales: vec4\<f32\>,  
    tex\_mults: vec4\<f32\>,  
    sun\_dir: vec3\<f32\>,  
    ambient\_color: vec3\<f32\>,  
    diffuse\_color: vec3\<f32\>,  
    specular\_color: vec3\<f32\>,  
    specular\_exponent: f32,  
    use\_diffuse\_alpha: u32,  
};  
@group(2) @binding(0) var\<uniform\> map\_info: MapInfoUniforms;

@fragment  
fn fs\_main(in: FragmentInput) \-\> @location(0) vec4\<f32\> {  
    // 1\. Sample the foundational base diffuse and distribution maps  
    let base\_color \= textureSample(t\_base, samp, in.uv\_base).rgb;  
    let dist \= textureSample(t\_dist, samp, in.uv\_base);  
      
    // 2\. Compute independent UV scales for DNTS using multiplicative scaling  
    let uv0 \= in.world\_pos.xz \* map\_info.tex\_scales.x;  
    let uv1 \= in.world\_pos.xz \* map\_info.tex\_scales.y;  
    let uv2 \= in.world\_pos.xz \* map\_info.tex\_scales.z;  
    let uv3 \= in.world\_pos.xz \* map\_info.tex\_scales.w;  
      
    let dnts0 \= textureSample(t\_dnts0, samp, uv0);  
    let dnts1 \= textureSample(t\_dnts1, samp, uv1);  
    let dnts2 \= textureSample(t\_dnts2, samp, uv2);  
    let dnts3 \= textureSample(t\_dnts3, samp, uv3);  
      
    // 3\. Execute Diffuse Compositing Mathematics  
    var final\_diffuse \= base\_color;  
    if (map\_info.use\_diffuse\_alpha \== 1u) {  
        // Unclamped additive sum modulated by distribution weights and explicit tex\_mults  
        let detail\_diff \= (dnts0.a \* dist.r \* map\_info.tex\_mults.x) \+  
                          (dnts1.a \* dist.g \* map\_info.tex\_mults.y) \+  
                          (dnts2.a \* dist.b \* map\_info.tex\_mults.z) \+  
                          (dnts3.a \* dist.a \* map\_info.tex\_mults.w);  
        final\_diffuse \= final\_diffuse \+ vec3\<f32\>(detail\_diff);  
    }  
      
    // 4\. Synthesize Tangent-Space Normals  
    // Extract RGB, convert  space to \[-1, 1\] vector space, apply OpenGL green channel inversion  
    var n0 \= dnts0.rgb \* 2.0 \- vec3\<f32\>(1.0); n0.g \= \-n0.g;  
    var n1 \= dnts1.rgb \* 2.0 \- vec3\<f32\>(1.0); n1.g \= \-n1.g;  
    var n2 \= dnts2.rgb \* 2.0 \- vec3\<f32\>(1.0); n2.g \= \-n2.g;  
    var n3 \= dnts3.rgb \* 2.0 \- vec3\<f32\>(1.0); n3.g \= \-n3.g;  
      
    // Perform linear blending based on raw distribution weights, followed by strict normalization  
    let blended\_tangent\_normal \= normalize(  
        (n0 \* dist.r) \+ (n1 \* dist.g) \+ (n2 \* dist.b) \+ (n3 \* dist.a)  
    );  
      
    // Transform tangent normal back to world space for lighting equations  
    let tbn \= mat3x3\<f32\>(in.tangent, in.bitangent, in.normal);  
    let world\_normal \= normalize(tbn \* blended\_tangent\_normal);  
      
    // 5\. Evaluate Illumination & Specular Calculations (Blinn-Phong Model)  
    let spec\_sample \= textureSample(t\_spec, samp, in.uv\_base);  
    let light\_dir \= normalize(map\_info.sun\_dir);  
    // Simplified view vector for editor camera mechanics  
    let view\_dir \= normalize(vec3\<f32\>(0.0, 1000.0, 0.0) \- in.world\_pos);   
    let half\_vector \= normalize(light\_dir \+ view\_dir);  
      
    // Calculate light incidence angles  
    let ndotl \= max(dot(world\_normal, light\_dir), 0.0);  
    let ndoth \= max(dot(world\_normal, half\_vector), 0.0);  
      
    // Combine base ambient and directional diffuse  
    let ambient \= final\_diffuse \* map\_info.ambient\_color;  
    let diffuse \= final\_diffuse \* map\_info.diffuse\_color \* ndotl;  
      
    // Specular mapping: RGB scales magnitude, Alpha acts as exponent modifier  
    let active\_exponent \= map\_info.specular\_exponent \* spec\_sample.a;  
    let spec\_term \= pow(ndoth, active\_exponent) \* spec\_sample.rgb \* map\_info.specular\_color;  
      
    // Integrate all terms into final fragment output  
    let final\_color \= ambient \+ diffuse \+ spec\_term;  
    return vec4\<f32\>(final\_color, 1.0);  
}

### **Field-roles map**

| mapinfo field | Type | Role in shader | Required? |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| resources.detailTex | string (path) | Base detail overlay (sampled at world UV / 2.0) | Optional fallback |
| resources.splatDistrTex | string (path) | Per-pixel channel weights (RGBA) determining DNTS visibility | Required for DNTS |
| resources.splatDetailNormalTex | string | The 4 DNTS BC3 normals providing geometry deltas | Required for DNTS |
| resources.splatDetailNormalDiffuseAlpha | bool / int | Toggles the shader branch dictating if the DNTS alpha channel modulates the ground diffuse | Optional (Defaults to 0\) |
| resources.specularTex | string (path) | Dictates per-pixel specular reflection magnitude (RGB) and exponent mapping (Alpha) | **Required when DNTS active** |
| resources.skyReflectModTex | string (path) | Provides a local cubemap environment texture for enhanced specular reflection | Optional |
| splats.texScales | float | Multiplier defining the per-channel UV repetition of the DNTS samples | Required for DNTS |
| splats.texMults | float | Scalar defining the absolute visual intensity multiplier of each corresponding DNTS layer | Required for DNTS |
| lighting.sunDir | vec4 | Dictates celestial lighting direction (XYZ) and shadow ray-march termination (W) | Required |
| lighting.groundAmbientColor | vec3 | Establishes the constant baseline illumination term across all fragments | Required |
| lighting.groundDiffuseColor | vec3 | Controls the color scaling applied to the Lambertian diffuse term | Required |
| lighting.groundSpecularColor | vec3 | Controls the color scaling applied to the base specular reflection term | Required |
| lighting.specularExponent | float | The global baseline Phong exponent, further modified by specularTex.A | Required |
| lighting.groundShadowDensity | float | Scalar controlling shadow attenuation and light blocking logic | Required |

### **Editor-preview deferrals**

1. **Exclusion of skyReflectModTex Environment Mapping**: The rendering overhead associated with cubemap loading and continuous reflection sampling provides insignificant authoring value during terrain splat painting and is skipped entirely to optimize wgpu performance.  
2. **Exclusion of Dynamic Self-Shadowing (groundShadowDensity)**: Complex cascading shadow maps (CSM) generated by terrain self-occlusion or static unit placement are bypassed in favor of a static, unshadowed directional Lambertian baseline to keep author focus on texture color.  
3. **Absence of Atmospheric Post-Processing**: The preview explicitly ignores exponential distance fog (fogStart, fogEnd, fogColor), void-water sub-surface darkening, and global cloud scattering volumes defined in the atmosphere block.  
4. **TBN Derivation Divergence**: Due to differences between the wgpu static terrain grid and the Recoil ROAM dynamically patched mesh, minor sub-pixel variations in normal vector interpolation will naturally manifest across steep vertices or sharp cliff faces.  
5. **Unclamped Splat Accumulation**: The shader intentionally allows additive saturation if a user authors a splat distribution where channel weights severely exceed an aggregate of 1.0, accurately reflecting the native engine's lack of mathematical normalization and ensuring authors visualize the blowout.

#### **Works cited**

1. EngineCrash \[1767\] at \[rts/Rendering/GL/VBO.cpp\] from rts ... \- GitHub, accessed May 18, 2026, [https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/spring/issues/837](https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/spring/issues/837)  
2. Crash in nvogl.dll · Issue \#216 · beyond-all-reason/RecoilEngine \- GitHub, accessed May 18, 2026, [https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/spring/issues/216](https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/spring/issues/216)  
3. See if the lack of texture query-LOD can be worked around · Issue, accessed May 18, 2026, [https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/RecoilEngine/issues/794](https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/RecoilEngine/issues/794)  
4. \[spring\] branch master updated (21f60eb \-\> ddcd67f) \- Debian, accessed May 18, 2026, [https://alioth-lists-archive.debian.net/pipermail/pkg-games-commits/2016-July/043058.html](https://alioth-lists-archive.debian.net/pipermail/pkg-games-commits/2016-July/043058.html)  
5. WIP changelog since the fork · Issue \#117 · beyond-all-reason/RecoilEngine \- GitHub, accessed May 18, 2026, [https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/spring/issues/117](https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/spring/issues/117)  
6. MapOrbitalStation/mapinfo.lua at main \- GitHub, accessed May 18, 2026, [https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/MapOrbitalStation/blob/main/mapinfo.lua](https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/MapOrbitalStation/blob/main/mapinfo.lua)  
7. Detail Normal Texture Splatting (DNTS) \- Spring RTS Engine, accessed May 18, 2026, [https://springrts.com/wiki/Mapdev:splatdetailnormals](https://springrts.com/wiki/Mapdev:splatdetailnormals)  
8. Advanced SpringRTS Mapping Guide by Beherith \- Google Docs, accessed May 18, 2026, [https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PL8U2bf-c5HuSVAihdldDTBA5fWKoeHKNb130YDdd-w/edit](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PL8U2bf-c5HuSVAihdldDTBA5fWKoeHKNb130YDdd-w/edit)
